"Do Not Bring Your Whole Self to Work": Against Authenticity Culture

2 min read · 344 words · Deep Dive #65 · 2026-03-30
Source: Marc Andreessen: Will a16z Go Public & Why Labour Displacement with AI is Wrong (20VC) · KB #13

In a throwaway line that deserves amplification, Andreessen advises: "Do not bring your whole self to work. Whatever you do, do not bring your whole self to work." This directly contradicts the dominant corporate culture narrative of the last decade, which has championed "authentic" workplaces where employees express their full identity.

Andreessen's reasoning is professional, not cynical. The question of whether you need to like the founders you invest in gets a firm "no." Great professional relationships don't require personal friendship. A VC who adds value in every engagement, maintains professional standards, and delivers results can have a highly productive relationship with a founder they never speak to socially. "I've seen that work many times and I think it's totally fine."

The Harry Truman quote — "If you need a friend, get a dog" — applies to professional contexts more broadly. The workplace is not your support group. Your co-workers are not your therapists. Your investors are not your friends. Trying to fulfill personal emotional needs through professional relationships distorts both the professional output and the personal experience. It creates dependencies, conflicts of interest, and disappointment when professional incentives inevitably diverge from personal attachments.

This connects to the earlier discussion about founder loneliness. Founders feel isolated because they can't confide in investors, employees, or the public — everyone has misaligned incentives. The solution isn't to pretend those incentive misalignments don't exist (which is what "bring your whole self to work" implicitly does). The solution is to acknowledge them, maintain professional boundaries, and find genuine emotional support in relationships that don't have professional stakes attached.

Andreessen isn't arguing against being decent, kind, or ethical at work. He's arguing against the therapeutic model of work — the idea that your job should provide meaning, community, identity, and emotional fulfillment. When work tries to be everything, it becomes a poor version of all of them. When work stays in its lane — value creation, professional excellence, economic exchange — it can be genuinely excellent at what it does, and you can find the rest of life elsewhere.
